core.when: When implementing custom commands that return non-OBBject types or when integrating third-party extensions with the platform
core.action: Verify consistent validation policy for both inputs and outputs - either implement matching validation for return types or explicitly document allowed bypass cases; do not rely on no_validate flag as a general exception mechanism

consequence.description: Asymmetric validation where parameters are strictly type-checked but return types can be bypassed causes silent type mismatches in command pipelines; downstream commands receiving unexpected types fail with confusing errors that are hard to trace to the source

core.when: When deploying multiple OpenBB versions in the same Python environment or when upgrading extensions across versions
core.action: Rely on singleton metaclass (ExtensionLoader, singletons) for shared extension state across version boundaries - verify extension registry is version-scoped or isolated

consequence.description: Extension singletons shared across OpenBB versions cause state corruption where newer version loads older version's extensions or vice versa, leading to incorrect data, failed commands, or silent wrong results that are difficult to diagnose

core.when: When building workflows that depend on MIC codes or country data for regulatory compliance or trade eligibility verification
core.action: Implement explicit cache invalidation or short TTL for MIC/country lookups; monitor ISO registry update announcements and trigger cache refresh on version changes; do not assume cached reference data is current

consequence.description: TTL-cached MIC codes and country data become stale when ISO registry updates, causing trades to be validated against deprecated exchange codes or country classifications leading to compliance violations or rejected orders

core.when: When implementing workflows that use extensions loaded via lazy entry_points (charting, quantitative, etc.)
core.action: Check extension credentials during workflow initialization rather than waiting for first command execution; call a pre-flight credential validation scan for each registered extensions before starting critical pipelines

consequence.description: Lazy-loaded extension credentials bypass validation until first use, causing silent failures deep in pipelines where missing credentials surface as confusing runtime errors rather than upfront configuration guidance, disrupting automated trading workflows

core.when: When implementing or refactoring volatility calculation in relative rotation analysis
core.action: Use rolling standard deviation as the volatility measure with square root scaling for annualization — do not substitute with exponentially weighted volatility or other methods that change the statistical interpretation of volatility values

consequence.description: Changing volatility calculation method alters the statistical properties of the risk measure, causing relative rotation charts to produce materially different RS/RR quadrant assignments that affect tactical allocation decisions

core.when: When implementing or refactoring momentum calculation in relative rotation analysis
core.action: Use log returns for momentum calculation due to time-additivity property enabling aggregation across periods — do not substitute with arithmetic returns which lack time-additivity and produce asymmetric momentum signals

consequence.description: Using arithmetic returns instead of log returns produces momentum signals that cannot be properly aggregated across time periods, leading to inconsistent RS/RR quadrant assignments and incorrect tactical allocation signals

core.when: When implementing or refactoring relative strength calculation in relative rotation analysis
core.action: Calculate relative strength ratio as asset-to-benchmark division to identify outperformance/underperformance trends — do not use absolute return momentum which ignores benchmark comparison essential for relative evaluation

consequence.description: Using absolute returns instead of relative strength ratio produces allocation signals that ignore benchmark comparison, causing assets to be misclassified in RS/RR quadrants and leading to suboptimal tactical allocation decisions

core.when: When implementing or refactoring performance measurement in portfolio analysis
core.action: Use Omega ratio for performance measurement as it captures full return distribution including higher moments and asymmetric payoffs — do not substitute with Sharpe ratio which assumes normal distribution and ignores asymmetric return profiles

consequence.description: Using Sharpe ratio instead of Omega ratio ignores tail risk and asymmetric payoffs, causing performance rankings that misrepresent investor utility and leading to allocation in strategies with unfavorable risk profiles

core.when: When configuring credentials for OpenBB platform deployment
core.action: Set credentials via ENV variables for production deployments; be aware that empty string ENV values override file values — verify that ENV variable values are non-empty or remove the variable entirely if file-based credentials are intended

consequence.description: Empty string ENV variables silently override file-based credentials, causing authentication failures in production where expected credentials are not loaded, leading to service unavailability

core.when: When modifying system settings during implementation in OpenBB platform
core.action: Attempt to modify SystemSettings instance properties after initialization — the instance is frozen and immutable; any configuration changes require creating a new instance

consequence.description: Attempting to modify frozen SystemSettings raises an error at runtime, causing unexpected exceptions in code that assumes mutable configuration and requiring code restructuring to use instance recreation patterns

core.when: When computing correlation matrices used in portfolio optimization
core.action: Verify stationarity of input time series before computing Pearson correlation — apply ADF or KPSS stationarity tests (BD-052) to verify statistical assumptions are met; if inputs are non-stationary, apply differencing or use Engle-Granger co-integration approach

consequence.description: Applying Pearson correlation to non-stationary series produces spurious correlation values that misrepresent asset relationships, leading to portfolio optimizations based on statistically invalid inputs and significant allocation errors

core.when: When using OpenBB Python API in DataFrame-first analysis workflows
core.action: Call to_df() explicitly on OBBject results before pandas-native operations — be aware that OBBject default output requires explicit conversion which may break pandas-dependent pipelines if conversion is omitted

consequence.description: Code written expecting DataFrame returns fails silently when OBBject results are passed directly, causing pandas operation errors that manifest as cryptic type errors in data analysis pipelines

core.when: When configuring provider-specific parameter filters using extra='allow'
core.action: Verify that configured filter parameters are actually applied to results — test with known parameter values and check that filtering takes effect; do not assume silent acceptance of unknown parameters means filters are working correctly

consequence.description: extra='allow' silently drops unknown parameters, creating false confidence that filters are applied when they are not; results appear filtered but contain data that should have been excluded, leading to incorrect analytical conclusions

core.when: When implementing or modifying input sanitization logic in CLI command history
core.action: Maintain credential sanitization that redacts API keys, passwords, and other sensitive tokens from command history files using the sanitize_input function in completer.py

consequence.description: Without credential sanitization, API keys and passwords are stored in plaintext in command history files, creating a security vulnerability where credentials can be extracted by anyone with file access

core.when: When implementing or modifying data fetcher logic for provider data
core.action: Use the Transform-Extract-Transform (TET) architecture separating provider-specific logic from standardized output via transform_query and related methods

consequence.description: Bypassing the TET architecture causes provider-specific data structures to leak into the output, breaking multi-provider support and causing downstream code that expects standardized formats to fail or produce incorrect results

core.when: When implementing or modifying HTTP client request logging for ClientSession
core.action: Obfuscate sensitive information in HTTP request URLs and headers before logging using the obfuscate_request_info function to prevent API credentials and tokens from appearing in logs or debugging output

consequence.description: Without HTTP request obfuscation, API credentials embedded in URLs and headers are visible in logs and debugging output, exposing sensitive authentication tokens to anyone with log access

core.when: When implementing or modifying package builder file write operations during extension builds
core.action: Use file locking (FileLock with fcntl) to prevent concurrent write corruption when PackageBuilder writes to shared filesystem during extension builds

consequence.description: Without file locking, concurrent extension builds interleave write operations, corrupting package files and producing non-functional or inconsistent build artifacts

core.when: When defining Pydantic models for API response schemas
core.action: Use the exclude_from_api extra kwarg to mark internal fields (raw provider data, debugging info) for removal from REST API responses via validate_output in router/commands.py

consequence.description: Without exclude_from_api, internal fields leak into API responses, exposing implementation details and provider raw data that should remain internal to clients

core.when: When implementing or modifying options chains properties for Greeks calculations
core.action: Maintain manual last_price override capability in OptionsChainsProperties to handle stale underlying price data from providers and verify accurate Greeks calculations

consequence.description: Without last_price override, stale underlying prices from providers propagate into Greeks calculations, causing materially incorrect delta, gamma, theta, and vega values that lead to poor trading decisions

core.when: When implementing or modifying parallel data fetching with amake_requests for multi-asset queries
core.action: Implement per-host throttling to prevent rate limit errors when using async batch HTTP requests for parallel data fetching

consequence.description: Without per-host throttling, concurrent batch requests trigger provider rate limits, causing request failures and incomplete data fetches that break strategy execution

core.when: When implementing error handling for queries that may return no results
core.action: Use EmptyDataError exception distinct from general OpenBBError to enable differentiated user messaging for legitimate no-results scenarios instead of catching each exceptions uniformly

consequence.description: Catching EmptyDataError uniformly with other exceptions prevents differentiated error handling, causing users to see failure messages for legitimate empty results instead of appropriate informational messages

core.when: When implementing or modifying system settings change detection
core.action: Use hash comparison to detect external modifications to settings files, maintaining the same hash algorithm and serialization format used by _compare_hash in system_service.py

consequence.description: Without hash comparison, external settings modifications go undetected when using file watching callbacks alone, causing the system to operate with stale configuration until next explicit reload

core.when: When implementing or modifying MCP settings validation
core.action: Support both JSON and tuple validation formats in MCPSettings validator via _validate_json_or_tuple to reduce configuration friction for users

consequence.description: Restricting MCPSettings to a single format breaks backward compatibility with existing configurations, causing validation failures for users who have configured settings in alternative formats

core.when: When calculating or displaying portfolio PnL in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework enforces PnL conservation (realized_pnl + unrealized_pnl = total_pnl) — this validation is currently missing; discrepancies between components and total will not be flagged

consequence.description: Without PnL conservation validation, backtest reports may show inconsistent PnL figures where realized gains plus unrealized gains do not equal total PnL, causing silent errors in strategy evaluation and potentially masking losses or phantom profits

core.when: When calculating portfolio PnL in backtesting
core.action: Implement PnL conservation validation: after every trade, assert that abs(realized_pnl + unrealized_pnl - total_pnl) < 0.001 and log a CRITICAL error if the check fails; add a reconciliation method to verify PnL components sum correctly at each bar

consequence.description: Without PnL conservation, backtest results show phantom profits or masked losses that do not reflect actual trading outcomes, leading to incorrect strategy selection and potential financial loss in live trading

core.when: When splitting historical data into training and testing sets for strategy development
core.action: Assume the framework enforces train/test time split integrity — this validation is currently missing; data leakage between train and test sets will not be detected or prevented

consequence.description: Without train/test split integrity validation, future data may leak into training sets causing look-ahead bias, making backtest results appear more profitable than actual live trading performance would be

core.when: When implementing train/test data splitting for strategy backtesting
core.action: Implement temporal split integrity checks: verify that train_end_date < test_start_date, verify no feature values use future data relative to their timestamp, and add a data_leakage_detection flag that raises an error if train data contains timestamps >= test_start_date

consequence.description: Without train/test integrity checks, strategies optimized on leaked data will show inflated backtest returns that do not generalize to live trading, causing unexpected losses when deploying to production

core.when: When generating and executing trading signals within bar-based backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework enforces bar execution timing — this capability is currently missing; signals and order placements within the same bar are timestamped identically without distinguishing signal generation from order execution

consequence.description: Without proper signal-execution timing, orders execute at the same bar's close price as the signal, eliminating the realistic slippage and delay that occurs in live trading where execution follows signal generation

core.when: When implementing bar-based backtesting signal processing
core.action: Implement bar execution timing model: separate signal_timestamp from execution_timestamp, add a bar_delay parameter (default=1) that defers order execution to the next bar, and require explicit opt-in to same-bar execution with documentation of assumptions

consequence.description: Without signal-execution timing delays, backtests assume instantaneous execution at signal prices, overestimating returns for strategies that rely on intrabar timing and creating live trading performance gaps

core.when: When modeling transaction costs in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework provides complete cost modeling — this capability is currently missing; only basic costs may be tracked while funding costs, margin interest, and borrow costs are not included in the cost model

consequence.description: Without complete cost modeling, strategies that involve borrowing, margin, or long-short positions show artificially high returns because funding costs are excluded, leading to significant live trading losses when actual costs are incurred

core.when: When implementing transaction cost modeling for backtesting
core.action: Implement complete cost model with these components: commission_fee, slippage_model, funding_cost_rate (for long-short), margin_interest_rate, and borrow_cost_rate; verify total_cost_per_trade = commission + slippage_estimate + funding_cost_accrual

consequence.description: Without complete cost modeling, backtests for long-short strategies exclude funding costs and show returns 2-5% higher than live trading, causing strategies to appear profitable when they are actually unprofitable after real costs

core.when: When tracking order status through its lifecycle in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework implements order lifecycle state machine — this capability is currently missing; orders do not transition through proper states (pending, filled, partial_fill, cancelled, rejected) and status remains static

consequence.description: Without order lifecycle tracking, partially filled or rejected orders are not detected, causing position mismatches between expected and actual holdings, leading to incorrect PnL calculations and unexpected exposure in live trading

core.when: When implementing order management in backtesting
core.action: Implement order lifecycle state machine with states: PENDING, PARTIAL_FILLED, FILLED, CANCELLED, REJECTED, EXPIRED; define valid state transitions, track fill_price and fill_quantity for each fill event, and assert position_estimate == sum(filled_quantities) at each step

consequence.description: Without order lifecycle management, partial fills are ignored and orders are treated as fully executed, creating phantom positions and incorrect exposure calculations that cause significant losses when live orders partially fill

core.when: When modeling carry costs for long-short strategies in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework handles funding cost modeling (carry) — this capability is currently missing; long positions and short positions are not charged/paid financing costs, and cross-currency carry is not tracked

consequence.description: Without funding cost modeling, long-short strategies show returns excluding borrow costs for shorts and interest income for longs, making strategies appear more profitable than they would be in live trading with actual financing costs

core.when: When implementing long-short strategy backtesting
core.action: Implement funding cost model: charge borrow_rate * abs(short_position_value) per day for short positions, credit lending_rate * long_position_value per day for long positions; for equities use benchmark borrow rate (e.g., Fed funds + spread), for futures use contract-specified carry

consequence.description: Without funding cost modeling, short equity strategies appear 3-8% more profitable annually because borrow costs are not deducted, leading to strategy selection based on inflated backtest results

core.when: When evaluating strategy robustness in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework provides backtest overfitting protection — this capability is currently missing; strategies are evaluated on single train/test split without walk-forward analysis or sample size validation

consequence.description: Without overfitting protection, strategies optimized on limited data with single train/test split appear profitable but fail in live trading, as parameters are overfitted to historical noise rather than genuine market patterns

core.when: When implementing strategy backtest validation
core.action: Implement walk-forward analysis with minimum 3 out-of-sample periods, enforce minimum_sample_size = 252 trading days (1 year) for equity strategies, calculate Sharpe ratio stability across folds (std < 0.3), and require p-value > 0.05 for strategy edge before deployment recommendation

consequence.description: Without overfitting protection, strategies that pass single backtests with high Sharpe ratios will likely fail in live trading, losing the capital allocated based on unreliable backtest performance metrics

core.when: When executing market orders in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework enforces fill assumptions for market orders — this capability is currently missing; market orders are assumed to fill fully at current price without volume or liquidity checks

consequence.description: Without fill assumption modeling, market orders in illiquid stocks fill instantly at the current price regardless of order size, overestimating execution probability and underestimating slippage in live trading scenarios

core.when: When implementing market order execution in backtesting
core.action: Implement fill assumption model: for market orders, calculate fill_ratio based on order_size / ADV (average daily volume), apply slippage = order_size * (1 - fill_ratio) * 0.5% estimate, and for orders with fill_ratio < 0.5 flag as high_risk_execution with WARNING log

consequence.description: Without fill assumption modeling, large market orders in thin stocks appear fully executable at current prices, causing backtests to underestimate execution risk and overestimate strategy returns by 10-30% for illiquid names

core.when: When calculating insurance liabilities in actuarial valuation
core.action: Assume the framework computes best estimate and risk margin for insurance contracts — the framework does not implement actuarial best estimate calculation or risk margin methodology

consequence.description: Without best estimate and risk margin calculation, the framework produces incorrect insurance liability valuations that fail actuarial reporting requirements and regulatory reserve adequacy tests

core.when: When processing ceded premiums and reinsurance recoveries
core.action: Implement external reinsurance contract mapping to route ceded premiums and claims to proper reinsurance accounts; include treaty_id, coverage_lines, and recovery_percentage fields in mapping configuration

consequence.description: Without reinsurance contract mapping, ceded premiums and recoveries are not properly allocated to treaty accounts, causing financial misstatements and failed audit reconciliations

core.when: When processing insurance premiums and calculating reinsurance recoveries
core.action: Assume the framework maps ceded premiums and claims to reinsurance contracts — the framework does not implement reinsurance contract mapping with treaty_id and recovery_percentage fields

consequence.description: Without reinsurance contract mapping, ceded premiums and recoveries are not properly allocated to treaty accounts, causing financial misstatements and failed audit reconciliations

core.when: When configuring catastrophe model parameters for catastrophe-exposed portfolios
core.action: Implement external cat model selection and calibration logic with vendor_name, model_version, return_period_exceedance_probability, and aggregate_loss_distribution fields; calculate net losses by applying treaty terms to gross losses

consequence.description: Without catastrophe model selection and calibration, cat-exposed portfolios have no loss estimation, leading to incorrect reinsurance pricing and inadequate catastrophe reserve allocation

core.when: When modeling insurance catastrophe losses
core.action: Assume the framework runs catastrophe models or processes cat model output — the framework does not implement cat model selection, calibration, or return period loss estimation (INS Coverage: 0/6)

consequence.description: Without catastrophe model support, cat-exposed portfolios have no loss estimation capability, leading to incorrect reinsurance pricing and inadequate catastrophe reserve allocation

core.when: When generating loan repayment schedules for lending products
core.action: Implement external repayment plan generation with principal_amount, interest_rate, term_months, payment_frequency, and amortization_type fields; calculate monthly_payment using standard amortization formula

consequence.description: Without repayment plan generation, loan schedules show incorrect payment allocations, causing customer billing errors and accounting mismatches between principal and interest

core.when: When managing loan products and customer payment schedules
core.action: Assume the framework generates repayment plans or amortization schedules — the framework does not implement repayment plan generation with principal_interest_split calculation

consequence.description: Without repayment plan generation, loan schedules show incorrect payment allocations, causing customer billing errors and accounting mismatches between principal and interest

core.when: When classifying loan portfolio by delinquency status and calculating provisions
core.action: Implement external delinquency classification with dpd_threshold field set to 30, 60, or 90 days; calculate roll rates by tracking accounts transitioning between delinquency buckets using migration_matrix

consequence.description: Without delinquency definition and roll rates, the framework misclassifies loan status, causing incorrect expected credit loss provisions and regulatory reporting violations

core.when: When classifying loan portfolio by delinquency status for provision calculation
core.action: Assume the framework classifies loan delinquency status or calculates roll rates — the framework does not implement dpd_threshold logic or migration_matrix for delinquency buckets (DPD 30/60/90)

consequence.description: Without delinquency definition and roll rates, the framework misclassifies loan status, causing incorrect expected credit loss provisions and regulatory reporting violations

core.when: When processing double-entry accounting transactions
core.action: Assume the framework maintains double-entry bookkeeping integrity or validates debit/credit balance — the framework does not implement balance_check or journal_entry_validation for accounting entries

consequence.description: Without double-entry bookkeeping integrity checks, unbalanced journal entries are accepted silently, causing material misstatements in financial statements and failed audit tests

core.when: When posting financial transactions to the general ledger
core.action: Implement external double-entry validation that checks sum_of_debits equals sum_of_credits for each journal entry; reject entries where total_debit != total_credit with error code GL-IMBALANCE

consequence.description: Without double-entry bookkeeping integrity checks, unbalanced journal entries are accepted silently, causing material misstatements in financial statements and failed audit tests

core.when: When calculating regulatory liquidity metrics for banking supervision
core.action: Assume the framework calculates LCR (liquidity coverage ratio) or NSFR (net stable funding ratio) — the framework does not implement HQLA classification, cash_flow_projection, or liquidity ratio formulas per Basel III

consequence.description: Without LCR and NSFR calculation, the framework cannot produce regulatory liquidity reports, causing supervisory reporting failures and potential banking license penalties

core.when: When reporting regulatory liquidity metrics to banking supervisors
core.action: Implement external LCR calculation: HQLA_divided_by Net_Cash_Outflows_30d >= 100%; implement NSFR calculation: Available_Stable_Funding / Required_Stable_Funding >= 100%; include hqla_tier_classification and cf Maturity_Bucket fields

consequence.description: Without LCR and NSFR calculation, the framework cannot produce regulatory liquidity reports, causing supervisory reporting failures and potential banking license penalties

core.when: When allocating internal funding costs between business units
core.action: Assume the framework calculates Fund Transfer Prices for internal transactions — the framework does not implement FTP_rate calculation or transfer_cost_allocation between business units

consequence.description: Without FTP method, internal fund transfers lack proper cost attribution, causing incorrect profitability measurement and suboptimal business unit performance evaluation

core.when: When allocating funding costs to business units for performance measurement
core.action: Implement external FTP calculation using matched-maturity or single-rate method with ftp_rate, fund_amount, maturity_date, and business_unit fields; allocate transfer_costs_proportionally based on duration-matched curves

consequence.description: Without FTP method, internal fund transfers lack proper cost attribution, causing incorrect profitability measurement and suboptimal business unit performance evaluation

core.when: When configuring cash pool structures for group treasury management
core.action: Assume the framework validates cash pool legal structures or checks notional vs physical pooling requirements — the framework does not implement cash_pool_type validation or regulatory structure compliance checks

consequence.description: Without cash pool legal structure validation, pooling arrangements may violate banking regulations, causing legal penalties and invalid tax treatment of intercompany transfers

core.when: When setting up group treasury cash pools for intercompany fund transfers
core.action: Implement external cash pool structure validation with pool_type (notional/physical), participant_entities, master_account_id, and regulatory_jurisdiction fields; verify pooling structure complies with local banking regulations before activation

consequence.description: Without cash pool legal structure validation, pooling arrangements may violate banking regulations, causing legal penalties and invalid tax treatment of intercompany transfers

core.when: When calculating bond prices, interest accruals, or performing any time-value-of-money computations in the quantitative analysis stage
core.action: Assume the framework implements day count conventions (30/360, Act/365, Act/360, etc.) or compounding frequency settings — the framework does not enforce any standard convention for these calculations

consequence.description: Without explicit day count convention enforcement, bond price calculations may differ between the framework and actual market pricing, causing systematic valuation errors in fixed income strategies that compound over time

core.when: When implementing or configuring any fixed income, interest, or time-value-of-money calculations in the quantitative analysis stage
core.action: Explicitly specify day count convention (e.g., 30/360, Act/365, Act/360, Act/Act) and compounding frequency for each calculation, documenting the chosen convention in strategy parameters

consequence.description: Unspecified day count conventions cause inconsistent interest accrual calculations across different instruments, leading to systematic pricing errors that accumulate in strategies holding multiple fixed income positions

core.when: When processing tax-advantaged account transactions (ISA, IRA, 401k, etc.) in portfolio operations
core.action: Assume the framework encodes tax-exempt period rules or annual contribution limits — the framework does not implement tax regulation encoding for tax-advantaged accounts

consequence.description: Without tax-exempt period rule encoding, backtests may include transactions that would violate contribution limits or exempt period restrictions in live trading, causing regulatory violations and potential penalties

core.when: When backtesting strategies involving tax-advantaged accounts (UK ISA, US IRA, etc.) or tax-exempt investment vehicles
core.action: Encode tax-exempt period rules by validating transaction dates against annual contribution limits and exempt period boundaries, filtering or rejecting transactions that would violate jurisdiction-specific tax regulations

consequence.description: Strategies that assume tax-advantaged execution without encoding contribution limits will produce non-reproducible live results, as transactions exceeding ISA/IRA limits are rejected or penalized in actual trading accounts

core.when: When using the to_df method to convert data in the results_container stage
core.action: Verify the input data contains a 'date' field before relying on the default index='date' parameter; if the date field has a different name (e.g., 'timestamp', 'datetime', 'time'), explicitly specify index='<actual_field_name>'

consequence.description: Default index='date' silently fails or produces incorrect DataFrame indexing when the actual data field is named differently, causing time-series plotting and analysis functions to produce misleading results

core.when: When adding a new provider fetcher or using the require_credentials parameter in openbb_core.provider.abstract
core.action: Verify the provider actually requires API credentials before changing require_credentials from the default True; for truly public data sources, explicitly set require_credentials=False to avoid unnecessary authentication failures

consequence.description: Default require_credentials=True causes silent authentication failures when adding public data providers, breaking backtesting workflows without clear error messaging about the security default

core.when: When implementing pricing models for options, convertible securities, or structured products
core.action: Assume the framework validates arbitrage-free conditions — the framework does not implement arbitrage-free constraint validation; models may produce theoretically impossible prices

consequence.description: Without arbitrage-free validation, pricing models can produce theoretically impossible prices (e.g., early exercise value violations in options), leading to systematic mispricing and trading losses when arbitrageurs exploit model violations

core.when: When implementing pricing validation for options, convertible securities, or structured products
core.action: Implement arbitrage-free constraint validation ensuring: put-call parity holds within tolerance (e.g., |C - P - S + K*e^(-rT)| < threshold), early exercise boundary is respected for American options, and no negative option premiums exist

consequence.description: Without arbitrage-free constraint validation, pricing models produce theoretically impossible prices that arbitrageurs can exploit; PRC coverage drops to 0/8 (0%), causing production pricing failures for regulated instruments

core.when: When processing corporate actions and tax-related events in backtesting
core.action: Assume the framework automatically binds event types to tax rules — the framework does not implement event type to tax rule binding; tax implications are not applied to corporate actions

consequence.description: Without event type to tax rule binding, dividend income, interest payments, and capital gains are not mapped to their corresponding tax treatment, causing backtest results to misrepresent after-tax returns and violating regulatory reporting requirements

core.when: When implementing corporate action and tax event processing
core.action: Implement event type to tax rule binding with a mapping table: dividend events map to dividend income tax rules, interest events map to interest income tax rules, capital gains events map to capital gains tax rules based on holding period

consequence.description: Without event type to tax rule binding, after-tax returns are miscalculated, leading to regulatory compliance violations and investor misrepresentation of actual strategy performance

core.when: When implementing or refactoring rolling statistics calculations
core.action: Implement rolling statistics computations over the entire dataset (no window parameter) — aggregate statistics must compute over the complete historical period, not a sliding window

consequence.description: Changing rolling statistics to use time-windowed calculations would fundamentally alter the metric interpretation, causing historical Sharpe/Sortino ratios to differ significantly from investor presentations and misrepresenting the strategy's long-term risk-adjusted performance

core.when: When implementing regression models for alpha estimation in CAPM or factor models
core.action: Include a constant term (intercept) in OLS regression using sm.add_constant() or equivalent — must NOT force regression through origin

consequence.description: Omitting the constant term forces regression through origin, producing biased alpha estimates when mean returns are non-zero; strategies based on biased alpha will underperform or generate losses

validation_threshold: X matrix does not include constant term AND regression_type == 'OLS' → FAIL

core.when: When implementing volatility estimation for stable market conditions
core.action: Use Garman-Klass estimator with open-high-low-close data for approximately 5x efficiency improvement over close-to-close; when only close prices are available, use close-to-close as fallback

consequence.description: Using close-to-close volatility when OHLC data is available underestimates true volatility in stable markets by ignoring overnight and intraday price range information, degrading strategy risk estimates

core.when: When implementing core services that require shared state management
core.action: Verify each classes using SingletonMeta remain process-local — distributed scenarios require external coordination mechanisms (distributed locks, external state stores)

consequence.description: Using SingletonMeta in distributed deployments (multi-process, container replicas) causes process-local singletons to diverge, resulting in inconsistent shared state and potential data corruption

core.when: When processing extension loading logic that requires portfolio rebalancing
core.action: Assume the framework provides a rebalancing trigger mechanism — the framework does not implement automatic portfolio rebalancing triggers; positions continue without rebalancing until manual intervention

consequence.description: Without rebalancing triggers, portfolio allocations drift from target over time, causing unintended risk exposure that compounds silently in live trading

core.when: When implementing portfolio rebalancing logic
core.action: Implement explicit rebalancing trigger checks using drift thresholds (e.g., allocation drift > 5% from target) or time-based intervals (e.g., monthly/quarterly rebalance calendar)

consequence.description: Without explicit trigger implementation, portfolio drift causes returns to diverge from expected risk-return profile; the gap in framework capability must be handled by application code

core.when: When implementing state persistence logic for extension configurations or data snapshots
core.action: Assume the framework provides versioned write operations or snapshot semantics — the framework does not implement version control for configuration writes or data snapshots; overwrites are destructive

consequence.description: Configuration updates or data snapshots occur without versioning, making rollback impossible when corrupted state is persisted and integrity verification becomes impossible

core.when: When implementing state persistence for extensions or data snapshots
core.action: Implement versioned write operations using atomic replace patterns (write-to-temp then rename) and maintain snapshot history with configurable retention (minimum 3 versions recommended)

consequence.description: Without versioned writes and snapshots, corrupted configurations cannot be rolled back to known-good state and data snapshots cannot be compared for integrity verification

core.when: When implementing command execution logic that passes parameters to functions
core.action: Call validate_kwargs before invoking target functions to enforce type checking and enable pydantic coercion — do not bypass or remove this validation layer during refactoring

consequence.description: Skipping kwargs validation causes type mismatches to surface as runtime errors with confusing stack traces instead of graceful coercion failures; silent type coercion from string to numeric may produce unexpected strategy parameters

core.when: When implementing beta estimation or expected return calculation using the CAPM framework
core.action: Use OLS regression on Fama-French factor data with at least 36 monthly observations; verify data meets OLS assumptions: linear relationship between market returns and asset returns, approximately normal residuals, and homoscedastic error terms

consequence.description: CAPM beta estimated with insufficient data or violated OLS assumptions produces unreliable risk estimates, causing misallocation of capital and strategies that underperform during market stress when beta accuracy matters most

validation_threshold: observation_count < 36 OR residuals_skewness > 1.0 OR jarque_bera_pvalue < 0.05 → FAIL

core.when: When implementing volatility estimation using the Yang-Zhang estimator
core.action: Use Yang-Zhang formula with k=0.34 as the optimal weighting parameter; require OHLC (open, high, low, close) price data; validate k parameter is in range [0.1, 0.9]

consequence.description: Yang-Zhang volatility requires all four OHLC price components; using only close prices omits the overnight and intraday variance components that provide 60-80% of total variance information, causing systematic underestimation of true volatility

validation_threshold: k not in [0.1, 0.9] OR OHLC_fields_missing → FAIL

core.when: When implementing volatility cones for term structure analysis
core.action: Use at least 3 rolling windows (e.g., [10, 20, 30] or [5, 21, 63] days) with at least 2 quantile levels (typically 10th and 90th percentiles) to construct the volatility cone; verify sufficient historical data covers the longest window

consequence.description: Volatility cones with fewer than 3 windows produce unreliable regime detection, causing mean-reversion strategies to enter at wrong volatility levels and miss profitable opportunities or enter overextended positions

validation_threshold: window_count < 3 OR quantile_count < 2 OR historical_data < max_window → FAIL

core.when: When implementing trend strength analysis using ADX in a backtesting strategy
core.action: Use ADX length=14 following Wilder's original specification — verify the length parameter is set to 14, not a rounded or alternative value like 7, 20, or 21

consequence.description: ADX with length=14 measures trend strength per Wilder's specification; using alternative lengths produces different trend classification results, causing the strategy to misclassify trending vs range-bound markets and execute inappropriate trades

core.when: When implementing momentum analysis using RSI for mean-reversion entry signals
core.action: Use RSI with 14-period window and 70/30 thresholds for overbought/oversold identification — verify the period is 14 and thresholds are exactly 70 (overbought) and 30 (oversold)

consequence.description: RSI with 70/30 thresholds identifies extreme readings for mean-reversion entry signals; using different thresholds like 80/20 reduces signal frequency and delays entries, while wrong period values alter momentum sensitivity causing false reversal signals

validation_threshold: rsi_period != 14 OR rsi_overbought != 70 OR rsi_oversold != 30 → WARN

core.when: When implementing cyclical analysis using CCI for mean-reversion strategies
core.action: Use CCI with 25-period length and ±100 thresholds following Lambert's original specification — verify the length is 25 and thresholds are ±100

consequence.description: CCI with 25-period and ±100 thresholds per Lambert's specification identifies cyclical turning points by measuring deviation from mean typical price; incorrect parameters produce false reversal signals, causing the strategy to enter positions at wrong market phases

core.when: When implementing timing analysis using Fisher Transform for reversal detection
core.action: Use Fisher Transform with ±2 thresholds (approximately 95% confidence interval) and 14-period length — verify thresholds are ±2 and period is 14

consequence.description: Fisher Transform ±2 thresholds correspond to 95% confidence interval in Gaussian-transformed distribution; different thresholds break this correspondence, causing unreliable extreme reading identification and timing applications to produce invalid reversal signals

core.when: When referencing prior command outputs in CLI workflows using OBBject registry
core.action: Remember that OBBject registry uses stack semantics where OBB0 is the latest result and OBB1 is the previous result — verify index interpretation matches this stack order, not FIFO queue or chronological order

consequence.description: OBBject registry stack semantics mean OBB0 is latest and OBB1 is previous; misinterpreting these as FIFO or chronological causes users to reference stale or wrong historical data when building complex workflows, leading to incorrect analysis conclusions

validation_threshold: No specific numerical parameter — behavioral semantic constraint

core.when: When defining QueryParams or data schemas for OpenBB provider integrations
core.action: Verify each parameter names are correct before submitting requests; enable validation logging to detect unknown parameters being silently ignored

consequence.description: Extra='allow' silently accepts unknown parameters without warnings; typos in field names (e.g., 'start_datte' instead of 'start_date') pass validation but use default values, causing silently incorrect results

core.when: When processing data from OpenBB providers with strict=False type coercion
core.action: Verify data types after retrieval and implement explicit type validation for numeric fields used in calculations; log any type conversions that occur

consequence.description: Lenient type coercion may hide data quality issues; implicit conversions can affect numeric precision in financial calculations, leading to cumulative rounding errors in backtesting results

core.when: When implementing autocorrelation testing for econometric analysis
core.action: For autocorrelation detection beyond lag-1, use Breusch-Godfrey test instead of Durbin-Watson; document the lag order being tested in analysis outputs

consequence.description: Durbin-Watson test only detects first-order autocorrelation and has a dead band region; higher-order autocorrelation patterns go undetected, leading to incorrectly concluding residual independence in time series models

core.when: When implementing residual autocorrelation testing in econometric analysis
core.action: Use Breusch-Godfrey LM test for multi-lag autocorrelation detection — do NOT substitute with Durbin-Watson test or Ljung-Box test without explicit justification

consequence.description: Using Durbin-Watson only detects first-order autocorrelation; Ljung-Box tests joint hypothesis without specific lag structure, leading to invalid hypothesis testing conclusions for higher-order serial correlation that can invalidate strategy econometric validation

core.when: When normalizing data for algorithms requiring bounded input ranges
core.action: Use Min-Max scaling to constrain outputs to [0,1] range — do NOT use Z-score normalization when bounded outputs are required by downstream algorithms

consequence.description: Z-score produces unbounded outputs that violate assumptions for bounded-range algorithms, causing incorrect probability outputs in sigmoid-activated neural networks and improper relative rotation chart scaling

core.when: When defining or accessing provider credentials in OpenBB platform
core.action: Use standardized credential naming format {provider_name.lower()}_{credential_name} — each environment variables and credential file entries must follow this lowercase convention

consequence.description: Credential lookups fail silently when case mismatches occur, causing authentication errors in case-sensitive credential stores that prevent data fetching without clear error messages

core.when: When interpreting cointegration test results in statistical analysis
core.action: Verify that Engle-Granger cointegration test results are interpreted in the context of embedded ADF assumptions — understand that p-values cascade through test chaining and may not reflect standalone ADF confidence levels

consequence.description: Silent test chaining in Engle-Granger creates misleading p-values; users interpreting cointegration results without understanding embedded ADF assumptions will make incorrect decisions about mean-reversion strategies

core.when: When normalizing financial data features for machine learning or statistical models
core.action: Use Z-score normalization for financial returns with outliers and skew rather than Min-Max scaling; verify distribution characteristics before selecting a normalization method and document the chosen approach for reproducibility

consequence.description: Arbitrary normalization selection without understanding financial data characteristics distorts feature distributions; Min-Max scaling on skewed returns creates artificial bounds that propagate errors through ML models and backtests

core.when: When organizing CLI commands with custom argument groups
core.action: Assume the framework supports arbitrary custom argument group names beyond argparse defaults without validation — conflicting or malformed group names will cause parsing failures in _build_custom_groups

consequence.description: Without validation for custom argument groups, CLI parsing fails silently or throws cryptic errors when group names conflict with existing conventions or argparse internal handling

core.when: When defining custom argument groups in CLI commands
core.action: Validate group names against a predefined allowlist before registration in _build_custom_groups; verify no duplicate group names exist and wrap group construction in try-except to provide clear error messages

consequence.description: Invalid group names silently break CLI parsing; proper validation with allowlists and exception handling prevents cryptic failures during argument processing

core.when: When deploying the application in multi-process environments
core.action: Assume singleton pattern works correctly for service classes (AuthService, SystemService, UserService) across multiple processes — each process will initialize its own singleton instance via __call__, breaking shared state assumptions

consequence.description: Singleton enforcement via __call__ in singleton.py:14 creates process-local instances; multi-process deployments result in inconsistent state across processes, causing authentication and user management failures

core.when: When deploying services in multi-process or distributed environments
core.action: Replace singleton pattern with dependency injection using shared state mechanisms (e.g., Redis, database-backed state) to verify consistent service state across processes; avoid relying on __call__ singleton enforcement for distributed deployments

consequence.description: Process-local singletons cause inconsistent authentication and user state in distributed deployments; dependency injection with shared state ensures all processes reference the same service instances

core.when: When storing or passing MIC/alpha_2 codes through Exchange/Country type objects
core.action: Assume Exchange and Country types always contain valid MIC/alpha_2 format values — when the stored value format differs from expected MIC/alpha_2, accessor methods (mic, acronym, name) will fail or return incorrect data

consequence.description: Exchange and Country inherit from str and use MIC/alpha_2 codes directly at exchange_utils.py:78; invalid format values cause accessor methods to return incorrect or malformed data, breaking downstream data processing

core.when: When validating MIC/alpha_2 codes before creating Exchange/Country objects
core.action: Validate code format before instantiation using regex patterns (MIC: 4 uppercase letters, alpha_2: 2 uppercase letters) and provide explicit conversion methods in Exchange/Country; raise ValidationError on format mismatch

consequence.description: Unvalidated MIC/alpha_2 codes in Exchange/Country objects cause silent data corruption; format validation with regex and explicit conversion prevents malformed codes from propagating through the system

core.when: When implementing or selecting volatility models for options pricing
core.action: Assume the framework implements volatility surface interpolation between strikes or maturities — the framework does not provide volatility surface interpolation capability; interpolated values will be missing or incorrect

consequence.description: Without volatility surface interpolation, skew and term structure effects cannot be properly captured; strategies relying on interpolated vol for strike selection or maturity interpolation will produce incorrect Greeks and suboptimal trade signals

core.when: When calibrating option pricing models to market prices
core.action: Implement external volatility surface interpolation using cubic spline or SABR model between available strikes and maturities before passing to the framework, and document the interpolation method used

consequence.description: Strategies using interpolated volatility without documented methodology may produce inconsistent results between backtesting and live trading if different interpolation methods are applied

core.when: When calibrating option pricing models to market prices
core.action: Assume the framework can compute implied volatility from market prices — the framework does not implement an implied volatility solver; attempting to invert market prices will fail or return incorrect values

consequence.description: Without an implied volatility solver, model calibration to real market data is impossible; backtests using assumed or static volatility will diverge significantly from live trading conditions

core.when: When calibrating option pricing models for backtesting
core.action: Pre-compute implied volatility externally using Newton-Raphson or bisection method (convergence tolerance: 1e-6) on observed bid/ask prices, then pass calibrated volatility values to the framework as inputs

consequence.description: Backtests using assumed volatility without calibration to market-implied vol will systematically misprice options, causing strategies to exhibit unrealistic P&L patterns

core.when: When implementing finite difference PDE solvers for option pricing
core.action: Assume the framework validates finite difference grid stability — the framework does not implement CFL (Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy) condition checks; unstable grids will produce divergent or NaN results

consequence.description: Without CFL validation, finite difference schemes may become numerically unstable, producing NaN values or wildly oscillating prices that corrupt backtest results

core.when: When configuring finite difference grids for PDE-based option pricing
core.action: Validate time step dt satisfies the CFL condition: dt <= dx² / (2D) for diffusion equations and dt <= dx / |v| for convection, where D is diffusion coefficient, dx is spatial step, and v is convection velocity; reject configurations failing this check

consequence.description: Failing to validate CFL before running finite difference schemes can produce numerical explosions that invalidate entire backtest runs and make results non-reproducible

core.when: When implementing credit risk models or ECL calculations under IFRS 9
core.action: Assume the framework implements IFRS 9 staging logic or default definition criteria — the framework does not provide credit stage classification; each positions will be treated as Stage 1 with 12-month ECL

consequence.description: Without IFRS 9 staging, lifetime ECL is never recognized for deteriorating credits, causing systematic underestimation of credit reserves and regulatory non-compliance

core.when: When calculating expected credit loss for IFRS 9 compliance
core.action: Implement external staging logic: Stage 1 for instruments with no significant increase in credit risk (SICR) since origination, Stage 2 for instruments with SICR but not credit-impaired, Stage 3 for credit-impaired or defaulted; apply 12-month ECL for Stage 1 and lifetime ECL for Stage 2 and Stage 3

consequence.description: Misclassifying credit stages leads to incorrect ECL calculations, resulting in regulatory penalties and understated credit risk provisions in financial statements

core.when: When generating sustainability reports or ESG disclosures
core.action: Assume the framework defines or calculates GHG emissions scope boundaries — the framework does not implement GHG scope classification; emissions data will be missing or incorrectly categorized as Scope 1

consequence.description: Without GHG scope boundary definition, sustainability reports will incorrectly classify emissions, violating GHG Protocol and potentially regulatory disclosure requirements

core.when: When calculating carbon footprint or GHG emissions for ESG reporting
core.action: Define explicit scope boundaries before any emissions calculation: Scope 1 (direct: company-owned sources), Scope 2 (indirect: purchased energy), Scope 3 (value chain: upstream/downstream); for compliance, at minimum Scope 1 and 2 must be reported per GHG Protocol; if Scope 3 is included, specify which of the 15 categories apply

consequence.description: Incorrectly categorizing emissions across scopes leads to non-compliant ESG disclosures, potential greenwashing claims, and regulatory sanctions under emerging carbon reporting rules

core.when: When implementing PCAF (Principles for Responsible Banking) aligned data processing
core.action: Assume the framework provides a built-in PCAF Data Quality Score calculation — this capability is missing from the framework; the framework only provides 1/6 (16.7%) CMP coverage

consequence.description: Without implementing PCAF Data Quality Score, the framework cannot produce compliant PCAF reporting, causing regulatory non-compliance and inability to meet UN Principles for Responsible Banking disclosure requirements

core.when: When implementing PCAF-aligned data processing for responsible banking reporting
core.action: Implement PCAF Data Quality Score calculation following the PCAF Global Banking Alliance methodology, scoring data quality across completeness, timeliness, accuracy, and consistency dimensions (DQA methodology requires scoring 0-100 for each quality indicator)

consequence.description: Without implementing PCAF Data Quality Score, the framework cannot produce compliant PCAF reporting, causing regulatory non-compliance and inability to meet UN Principles for Responsible Banking disclosure requirements

core.when: When implementing reinforcement learning trading agent reward functions
core.action: Assume the RL reward function automatically incorporates trading costs — this capability is missing; RL agents will optimize gross returns without accounting for transaction costs

consequence.description: RL agents trained with incomplete reward functions systematically overestimate net returns; in live trading, the agent will execute trades that appear profitable in backtest but are loss-making after costs, causing significant financial loss

core.when: When implementing RL-based trading strategies with reinforcement learning agents
core.action: Explicitly include trading costs (commission fees, slippage, bid-ask spread) in the reward function calculation: reward = gross_pnl - commission_cost - slippage_cost - spread_cost, ensuring RL agent optimizes net P&L rather than gross returns

consequence.description: RL agents trained with incomplete reward functions systematically overestimate net returns; in live trading, the agent will execute trades that appear profitable in backtest but are loss-making after costs, causing significant financial loss

core.when: When implementing ML pipeline transformation logic for feature engineering
core.action: Assume the framework automatically handles fit/transform isolation between training and inference — this capability is missing; pipelines will leak information if not explicitly controlled

consequence.description: Without fit/transform isolation, scalers and encoders learn from test data during fit, causing look-ahead bias that inflates backtest performance and produces non-reproducible results in live trading

core.when: When implementing ML pipeline preprocessing stages for trading signal generation
core.action: Explicitly separate fit operations from transform operations: use sklearn Pipeline with ColumnTransformer or equivalent, fit transformers only on training split, apply learned parameters to test/inference splits, preventing any information leakage across data splits

consequence.description: Without fit/transform isolation, scalers and encoders learn from test data during fit, causing look-ahead bias that inflates backtest performance and produces non-reproducible results in live trading

core.when: When implementing multi-agent trading systems with multiple AI agents
core.action: Assume the framework provides built-in consensus mechanisms for multi-agent decision-making — this capability is missing; agents will produce independent signals without aggregation

consequence.description: Without consensus mechanisms, conflicting agent signals are not reconciled, causing erratic trading behavior where the system may execute contradictory orders simultaneously

core.when: When implementing multi-agent trading architectures with multiple AI agents
core.action: Implement explicit consensus mechanisms to aggregate multi-agent signals: use majority voting, weighted voting with confidence scores, or auction-based aggregation, with defined fallback strategy when agents disagree (AIL Coverage target: 6/6)

consequence.description: Without consensus mechanisms, conflicting agent signals are not reconciled, causing erratic trading behavior where the system may execute contradictory orders simultaneously

core.when: When implementing insurance reserve estimation for actuarial calculations
core.action: Assume the framework provides built-in reserve adequacy calculation methods (Chain Ladder, Bornhuetter-Ferguson, Mack) — this capability is missing; basic IBNR calculations will be inaccurate

consequence.description: Without reserve adequacy methods, the framework cannot properly calculate IBNR reserves, leading to under-reserving that causes capital inadequacy or over-reserving that triggers unnecessary capital requirements

core.when: When implementing insurance reserve estimation for actuarial analysis
core.action: Implement reserve adequacy methods following standard actuarial practices: Chain Ladder method for incurred claims development factors, Bornhuetter-Ferguson method for a priori loss estimation, Mack method for stochastic uncertainty quantification with confidence intervals

consequence.description: Without reserve adequacy methods, the framework cannot properly calculate IBNR reserves, leading to under-reserving that causes capital inadequacy or over-reserving that triggers unnecessary capital requirements
